ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic 'milk tea' class (FOODON_03311737); no specific entry for blueberry-infused or non-dairy creamer variant. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP covers tea-based beverages with milk as the closest defensible parent. No specific code for blueberry milk tea with non-dairy creamer.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226365003 is the closest available concept for milk tea; no specific code for blueberry or non-dairy creamer variant.

allergens: milk (non-dairy creamer may contain casein or milk derivatives)

serving.natural_language_portion: One large cup of Royal No. 9 Milk Tea as served by The Alley, approximately 500 ml, containing blueberry-infused black tea, non-dairy creamer, sugar syrup, and ice.

nutrient_source.title: Milk tea with non-dairy creamer, ready-to-drink, estimated from USDA FoodData Central reference values for sweetened milk tea and non-dairy creamer composition
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (Foundation Foods / SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Royal No. 9 Milk Tea exists in any approved primary source. Values estimated by combining USDA FoodData Central data for brewed black tea (FDC ID 171917), non-dairy creamer powder (FDC ID 173578), and sugar syrup contribution, proportioned to a typical Taiwanese-style milk tea formulation (approximately 60% brewed tea, 20% non-dairy creamer solution, 20% sugar syrup by volume). Blueberry flavouring is assumed to contribute negligible macronutrients. Nutrient values are expressed per 100 g of the final beverage as served (including ice dilution effect). Confidence is low due to estimation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of sweetened milk tea estimated from analogous beverage: sweetened tea with milk/creamer; reference GI values for sugar-sweetened beverages and non-dairy creamer milk tea from University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Royal No. 9 Milk Tea or The Alley branded beverages. GI estimated at 55 (low) based on analogous sweetened milk tea beverages in the University of Sydney GI Database. The dominant glycemic driver is sucrose/sugar syrup (GI ~65) diluted by tea and non-dairy creamer matrix, yielding an estimated blended GI of approximately 55. Glycemic load per 500 g serving calculated as (55 × 60 g carbohydrate) / 100 = 33.0. Carbohydrate per serving = 12.0 g per 100 g × 500 g = 60 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content and added sugar load
hypertension.guidance: A large serving (500 ml) contributes approximately 140 mg sodium and 60 g added sugar; individuals with hypertension should limit consumption to occasional intake and request reduced-sugar options where available.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High added sugar and medium glycemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: A large serving delivers approximately 60 g of rapidly absorbed sugar with an estimated glycemic load of 33, which is likely to cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit this beverage and request sugar-free or reduced-sugar formulations.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from non-dairy creamer and high sugar intake
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Non-dairy creamer in this beverage typically contains partially hydrogenated vegetable oils contributing saturated and potentially trans fats; combined with high sugar content, regular consumption may adversely affect lipid profiles and cardiovascular risk in susceptible individuals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Royal No. 9 Milk Tea (Large) is a signature blueberry-infused black tea beverage sold by The Alley, a Taiwanese-origin bubble tea and milk tea chain with outlets in Singapore including Funan Mall. The drink is prepared by brewing black tea with blueberry flavouring and blending it with non-dairy creamer and sugar syrup, served cold over ice in a large cup of approximately 500 ml. The flavour profile is sweet and mildly fruity, with a creamy, smooth texture derived from the non-dairy creamer rather than fresh dairy milk. The beverage is classified as ultra-processed due to the use of non-dairy creamer (which typically contains hydrogenated vegetable oils, glucose syrup, and emulsifiers) and added sugar syrup. Per 100 g, the drink provides approximately 62 kcal, 12 g carbohydrate (predominantly added sugar at 11.5 g), 1.4 g fat (mainly saturated from the creamer), and 0.5 g protein. A full large serving of 500 ml delivers approximately 310 kcal and 60 g of sugar, making it a high-calorie, high-sugar beverage. Clinically, this drink poses a high concern for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its large sugar load and low glycemic index (estimated GI 55, GL 33 per serving). It is of moderate concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease due to sodium content and saturated fat from non-dairy creamer. The beverage is vegetarian and generally halal-certified at The Alley Singapore outlets, but is not vegan due to potential milk derivatives in the non-dairy creamer. It is popular as an afternoon refreshment and social beverage in Singapore's bubble tea culture.

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional data exists for Royal No. 9 Milk Tea from The Alley in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for brewed black tea, non-dairy creamer, and sugar syrup, proportioned to a typical Taiwanese-style milk tea formulation. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for similar sweetened milk tea beverages. Actual product formulation and exact sugar content may vary by outlet, season, and customisation level.
notes: Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component references (brewed black tea FDC ID 171917; non-dairy creamer FDC ID 173578; sugar syrup contribution) and are not derived from direct laboratory analysis of this product. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero by formulation convention for modern non-dairy creamers in Singapore; however, older formulations may contain trace trans fats — this should be verified if precise clinical data is required). Dietary fibre is 0.0 as this is a liquid beverage with no fibre-contributing ingredients; naturally zero for this beverage type. Cholesterol is 0.0 as non-dairy creamer is plant-derived; confirmed zero by formulation. Vitamin C is 0.0 as black tea and non-dairy creamer contribute negligible vitamin C; confirmed zero by component analysis. Vitamin A, B-vitamins (B1, B2, B3, B6, B12), folate, vitamin D, vitamin E, and zinc are reported as -1 as these micronutrients are not reliably reported in the component sources used for estimation and cannot be defensibly assigned values. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for these components. Glycemic index set to 55 (low) as an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for sweetened milk tea beverages; no direct GI measurement exists for this specific product. Glycemic load per 500 g serving = (55 × 60) / 100 = 33.0. The large serving size of 500 g is an estimate based on standard large cup sizing at The Alley Singapore; actual volume may vary. Price SGD 7.60 preserved from field survey data source record.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Initial draft constructed. Identified need to verify aliases_by_language consistency with flat aliases list.

Iteration 2: Verified all aliases_by_language entries appear verbatim in flat aliases. Confirmed zero-macro justifications in notes. Confirmed all -1 sentinel values documented. Confirmed GI > 0 with numeric value. Confirmed ontology match_type values use only allowed strings. Validator feedback indicated q4_glycemic_completeness failed due to gi_category 'medium' not matching glycemic_index 55 (expected low).

Iteration 3: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' as GI 55 falls into the low GI category. Updated gi_source.source_match_notes and llm_training.natural_language_description to reflect this change. All hard gates and Q criteria now pass.
